narration.text: Every program needs memory. And for decades, we've had two ways to manage it, both with serious trade-offs.

In C and C-plus-plus, you manage memory manually. You call malloc to allocate and free to release. Full control, but one mistake — a forgotten free, a use-after-free — and you've got a crash, a security hole, or worse.

Languages like Java, Python, and Go took the opposite approach: a garbage collector. It tracks what's in use and cleans up automatically. Safe, but you pay with unpredictable pauses and higher memory usage.

Rust introduced a third way: ownership. The compiler itself tracks who owns each piece of memory and inserts the cleanup code at compile time. Zero runtime cost. Zero garbage collector. And if your code compiles, memory bugs simply can't happen.
The entire ownership system rests on three rules. Rule one: each value in Rust has exactly one owner. Not two, not zero — one variable owns that data at any given time.
Rule two: there can only be one owner at a time. When ownership transfers, the previous owner can no longer access the value. This is fundamentally different from most languages you've used.
Rule three: when the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. Rust calls a special drop function automatically. No garbage collector needed — the compiler knows exactly when to clean up.
Now here's where it gets interesting. Let's say you have a String, s1, and you write let s2 equals s1. In most languages, you'd expect both variables to point to the same data. But what does Rust do?

Think about it for a moment. If both s1 and s2 owned the same heap data, what would happen when both go out of scope? Rust would try to free the same memory twice — a double free bug. So instead, Rust moves the ownership.

When you assign s1 to s2, Rust doesn't copy the heap data and it doesn't create a second pointer. It transfers ownership. s1 is invalidated — you can't use it anymore. The compiler enforces this. If you try to use s1 after the move, you get a compile error, not a runtime crash.

Let me show you the code. Before the move, s1 has a pointer, a length, and a capacity on the stack, pointing to hello on the heap. After let s2 equals s1, those stack values move to s2, and s1 is marked invalid. Only s2 can access the heap data now.

But wait — not everything moves. Simple types like integers, floats, booleans, and characters implement the Copy trait. When you write let y equals x where x is an i32, Rust copies the value. Both x and y remain valid.

The rule is straightforward: if a type lives entirely on the stack and copying it is cheap, it implements Copy. Integers, floats, booleans, chars, and tuples of Copy types. Anything that allocates heap memory — like String, Vec, or Box — cannot be Copy. It must move.
Moving is safe, but it's restrictive. What if a function just needs to read a value without taking ownership? That's where borrowing comes in. Instead of giving away ownership, you lend a reference.

A shared reference, written ampersand T, lets you read the data but not modify it. You can have as many shared references as you want — just like multiple readers at a library. The original owner keeps ownership.

A mutable reference, written ampersand mut T, lets you both read and modify the data. But there's a catch: you can only have one mutable reference at a time, and you can't have any shared references while a mutable reference exists.
Think of it this way: many readers, or one writer. Never both at the same time. This is the borrowing rule that prevents data races at compile time.

The borrow checker is the part of the Rust compiler that enforces these rules. It analyzes your code at compile time, tracking every reference and its lifetime. If you violate any borrowing rule, your code won't compile.

Here's a classic example. You create a mutable reference r1 to a vector, then try to create a shared reference r2 while r1 is still active. The borrow checker rejects this — you can't have a shared reference while a mutable reference exists.

But the borrow checker is smarter than a simple rule engine. It tracks when references are actually used — not just when they're declared. If r1's last use is on line three, and r2 is created on line five, the borrow checker sees that their lifetimes don't overlap, and the code compiles fine. This is called Non-Lexical Lifetimes, or NLL.

Speaking of lifetimes — sometimes the compiler needs help figuring out how long a reference is valid. That's what lifetime annotations are for. They don't change how long something lives — they tell the compiler about the relationships between references.

Consider a function that takes two string slices and returns one of them. The compiler asks: does the returned reference live as long as the first input, the second, or both? You answer with lifetime annotations. The tick a in ampersand tick a str tells the compiler: the output lives at least as long as both inputs.

Most of the time, Rust infers lifetimes through three elision rules, and you never write an annotation. But when a function returns a reference and has multiple inputs, you need to be explicit. The compiler will always tell you when.

Now let's zoom out and see what this system actually prevents. Ownership isn't just an academic exercise — it eliminates entire categories of bugs that plague C, C-plus-plus, and even garbage-collected languages.
Dangling pointers — when you use a reference after the data it points to has been freed. Ownership prevents this because the compiler ensures references never outlive the data they point to.
Double free — when you free the same memory twice. Ownership prevents this because exactly one owner calls drop, and moved values can't be used again.

Data races — when two threads access the same data and at least one is writing. The borrowing rules prevent this at compile time: you can't have a mutable reference and any other reference at the same time, period.

Let's bring it all together. Three rules — one owner, transfer on assignment, drop on scope exit. Borrowing extends this with shared and mutable references. The borrow checker enforces everything at compile time. And lifetimes make the relationships explicit when the compiler needs help.

The result? Memory safety without a garbage collector. No runtime cost. No pauses. If it compiles, it's safe. That's Rust's ownership promise — and once you internalize these rules, you'll find that the compiler isn't fighting you. It's catching bugs you didn't even know you had.

q-move.title: Predict the Move
q-move.desc: You write: let s2 = s1; where s1 is a String. What happens next?
options: Both s1 and s2 point to the same heap data (shared ownership)
Rust copies the heap data so s1 and s2 each have their own copy
s1 is invalidated — only s2 can access the data (ownership moved)

correct.title: Exactly right
correct.body: Rust moves ownership to s2 and invalidates s1. This prevents double-free bugs — only one variable can drop the heap data.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Shared ownership would cause a double-free when both go out of scope. Copying heap data would be expensive and implicit. Instead, Rust moves ownership — s1 becomes invalid, only s2 can use the data.

try-borrow-rules.title: Valid or Invalid Borrows?
try-borrow-rules.desc: Drag each code pattern into the correct category.

categories.label: Compiles
items: Multiple &T refs to the same value
One &mut T ref with no &T refs
&T ref after &mut T is last used

categories.label: Rejected
items: Two &mut T refs at the same time

correct.body: You've got the borrowing rules down. Many readers OR one writer — never both. And NLL means the compiler tracks actual usage, not just declaration scope.

wrong.body: Remember: any number of &T (shared) refs are fine together. Only one &mut T at a time, and no &T refs can coexist with &mut T. The compiler tracks when refs are actually used (NLL).

q-borrow-checker.title: Does This Compile?
options: Error: can't borrow v as shared while mutably borrowed
Compiles: r1's last use is before r2 is created (NLL)
Error: can't borrow v at all after pushing to it

correct.title: Correct
correct.body: Non-Lexical Lifetimes in action — r1 is last used on the push line, so its borrow ends there. By the time r2 borrows v, there's no conflict.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Before NLL (Rust 2015 edition), this would fail. But modern Rust tracks actual last usage: r1 is done after push(4), so the mutable borrow ends. r2's shared borrow starts after, so there's no overlap.

try-lifetime-scope.title: Lifetime Scope Explorer
try-lifetime-scope.desc: Slide to see how reference lifetimes affect what compiles.

stops.label: Line 1: let x = String::from("hi")
stops.detail: x is created. Its lifetime begins. It owns the heap-allocated string.

stops.label: Line 2: let r = &x
stops.detail: r borrows x. r's lifetime begins. x must outlive r for the borrow to be valid.

stops.label: Line 3: println!("{}", r)
stops.detail: r is used here. This is r's last use — under NLL, r's borrow ends after this line.

stops.label: Line 4: drop(x)
stops.detail: Compiles! r's borrow already ended at line 3. x can be safely dropped.

stops.label: What if r was used on line 5?
stops.detail: Error! x was dropped on line 4, but r tries to use it on line 5. The borrow checker catches this: x doesn't live long enough.

stops.label: Fix: move drop(x) after last use of r
stops.detail: The fix is simple — ensure the owner outlives all its borrows. The compiler tells you exactly where the conflict is.

try-bug-categories.title: Match the Bug to the Fix
try-bug-categories.desc: Drag to order: which ownership rule prevents each bug?
sortItems: One owner → drop is called exactly once (prevents double free)
Move semantics → invalidates old variable (prevents use-after-move)
References must outlive their borrows (prevents dangling pointers)
One writer XOR many readers (prevents data races)

correct.body: That's the ownership bug-prevention chain — from single ownership through move semantics, borrow checking, and the aliasing rule.

wrong.body: The chain builds on itself: single ownership enables safe drops, moves prevent stale access, lifetime checking prevents dangling pointers, and borrowing rules prevent data races.

q-synthesis.title: Why Is This Safe?
options: The garbage collector keeps buffer alive until process finishes
process borrows buffer as &[u8] — shared ref doesn't take ownership, so buffer remains valid
Rust copies buffer into process, so both the original and the copy are valid

correct.title: Perfect
correct.body: process takes a shared borrow (&[u8]), so buffer keeps ownership. After process returns, the borrow ends and buffer is still valid. No GC, no copy — just the borrow checker ensuring safety at compile time.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Rust has no garbage collector, and it doesn't implicitly copy heap data. The key is borrowing: process(&buffer) creates a shared reference. buffer keeps ownership, process can read but not modify, and after the call buffer is still valid. The borrow checker verifies all of this at compile time.
